THIS DISCOVERY DISPUTE IS PRESENTED TO ME ON THE LAST COURT DAY BEFORE TRIAL. THE JUDGE PRO TEM'S REPORT STATES: "THE ONLY LIVE DISPUTE IS PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO REQUEST FOR ADMISSION NOS. 18 AND 19." THOSE RFAS SEEK INFORMATION AS TO WHETHER A DEFENDANT POLICE OFFICER WAS REPRIMANDED OR DISCIPLINED FOR PLAINTIFF'S ARREST - AN ARREST ALLEGEDLY ACCOMPANIED BY USE OF FORCE THAT RESULTED IN GREAT BODILY INJURY. PENAL CODE 832.7 WAS RECENTLY AMENDED TO PROVIDE GREATER PUBLIC ACCESS TO POLICE MISCONDUCT RECORDS, INCLUDING RECORDS REGARDING USE OF FORCE THAT RESULTED IN GREAT BODILY INJURY. THE JPT'S REPORT RELIES ON AUTHORITIES THAT PRE-DATE THE RECENT AMENDMENTS AND DOES NOT INDICATE THAT THE AMENDMENTS HAVE BEEN CONSIDERED. I THEREFORE CONTINUE THIS DISPUTE FOR CONSIDERATION BY THE TRIAL JUDGE. JUDGE: RICHARD B. REPLY FOR SEPARATE STATEMENT OF UNDISPUTED FACTS IN SUPPORT OF MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (TRANSACTION ID # 65925471) FILED BY DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO NEARY, SEAN

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, SEP-18-2020 HEARING HELD RE: DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO AND SEAN NEARY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. ONE ELEMENT OF A RALPH ACT CLAIM IS THAT "A SUBSTANTIAL MOTIVATING REASON FOR DEFENDANT'S CONDUCT WAS HIS PERCEPTION OF PLAINTIFF'S POLITICAL AFFILIATION" OR OTHER PROTECTED CHARACTERISTIC. (CACI 3064(2).) DEFENDANTS HAVE EVIDENCE OF THEIR OWN ON THIS ELEMENT (UMF 37), AND SHOW AN ABSENCE OF EVIDENCE FROM PLAINTIFF. THE TAC PLEADS PLAINTIFF'S "POLITICAL AFFILIATION AND/OR GENDER." (ID. AT 5:23-25.) HOWEVER, GENDER IS NOT MENTIONED AS A MOTIVATING REASON IN THE OPPOSITION MEMORANDUM, SO THAT THEORY IS APPARENTLY ABANDONED. IT IS ALSO UNCLEAR WHAT "POLITICAL AFFILIATION" IS ALLEGED - THE TAC'S RALPH ACT COUNT IS SILENT, AND THE MEMO MERELY SAYS POLICE OFFICERS "PERCEIVED THE PROTESTORS AS BEING ANTI-LAW ENFORCEMENT AND ANTI-GOVERNMENT." (OPP. 17:6-7.) PLAINTIFF CITES TWO OFFICER DEPOSITIONS (NEITHER BY DEFENDANT NEARY) FOR THAT ASSERTION (PAMF 14), BUT ONE SAYS NOTHING OF THE SORT. (BELL INDEX EX. F PP. 12-13.) THE OTHER OFFICER RECALLED "KIND OF ANTIGOVERNMENT RHETORIC" BY PROTESTORS. (ID. AT EX. D P. 16.) THIS VAGUE DEPOSITION CLIP IS NOT ENOUGH TO AVOID SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF THE RALPH ACT CLAIM - AND IT IS THUS GRANTED. SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS DENIED ON THE BANE ACT CLAIM. ORDER SIGNED. Atty. CHRISTOPHER B. WHITMAN, Esq. of the San Francisco Office of the City Attorney
(Tel: 415-554-4240) for Defendants City and County of San Francisco and Officer Sean Neary.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF SUSAN BELL's Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant Neary.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. This discovery dispute is presented to me on the last court day before trial. The judge pro tem's report states: "The only live dispute is Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to Request for Admission Nos. 18 and 19." Those RFAs seek information as to whether a defendant police officer was reprimanded or disciplined for plaintiff's arrest - an arrest allegedly accompanied by use of force that resulted in great bodily injury. Penal Code 832.7 was recently amended to provide greater public access to police misconduct records, including records regarding use of force that resulted in great bodily injury. The JPT's report relies on authorities that pre-date the recent amendments and does not indicate that the amendments have been considered. I therefore continue this dispute for consideration by the trial judge.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Sep-18-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Attys. BETZY Y. BRAS-GONZALEZ, Esq. and MICHAEL R. BRACAMONTES, Esq. of Bracamontes & Vlasak, P.C. (Tel: 415-835-6777) for Plaintiff Susan Bell;
Atty. CHRISTOPHER B. WHITMAN, Esq. of San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Tel: 415-554-4240) for Defendants Officer Sean Neary and the City and County of San Francisco.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO AND SEAN NEARY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. One element of a Ralph Act claim is that "a substantial motivating reason for defendant's conduct was his perception of plaintiff's political affiliation" or other protected characteristic. (CACI 3064(2).) Defendants have evidence of their own on this element (UMF 37), and show an absence of evidence from plaintiff. The TAC pleads plaintiff's "political affiliation and/or gender." (Id. at 5:23-25.) However, gender is not mentioned as a motivating reason in the opposition memorandum, so that theory is apparently abandoned. It is also unclear what "political affiliation" is alleged - the TAC's Ralph Act count is silent, and the memo merely says police officers "perceived the protestors as being anti-law enforcement and anti-government." (Opp. 17:6-7.) Plaintiff cites two officer depositions (neither by defendant Neary) for that assertion (PAMF 14), but one says nothing of the sort. (Bell Index Ex. F pp. 12-13.) The other officer recalled "kind of antigovernment rhetoric" by protestors. (Id. at Ex. D p. 16.) This vague deposition clip is not enough to avoid summary adjudication of the Ralph Act claim - and it is thus granted. Summary adjudication is denied on the Bane Act claim. (See Word minutes for complete ruling.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records And Or For Protective Order, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Jan-09-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Betzy Bras-Gonzalez, Esq. of BRACAMONTES & VLASAK, T:415.835.6777, for plaintiff/moving party;
Christopher B. Whitman, Esq., Deputy City Attorney, T:415.554.4240, for defendants/opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF SUSAN BELL'S Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records And Or For Protective Order, modified, as follows:

Plaintiff's motion to quash deposition subpoenas for production of business records and/or for protective order is granted in part. Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is granted in the amount of $1,000. Defendant's motion for sanctions is denied.

This lawsuit arises out of an incident that occurred on July 9, 2018, when Plaintiff was arrested by the San Francisco Police Department for allegedly refusing to follow orders to vacate a public street. Plaintiff claims to have sustained physical injuries to her left shoulder and elbow in the arrest that have required her to undergo surgery. Defendant's subpoenas seek the production of "[a]ny and all medical records pertaining to plaintiff Susan Bell, including but not limited to medical treatment records, from 7/9/2008 to 7/9/2018," as well as billing records and radiology images for the same ten-year period. The subpoenas are overbroad and threaten to invade Plaintiff's right to medical privacy with respect to unrelated medical conditions and treatments. A personal injury plaintiff's waiver "extends only to information relating to the medical conditions in question, and does not automatically open all of a plaintiff's past medical history to scrutiny." (Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 849.) The scope of the inquiry permitted depends upon the nature of the injuries which the patient-litigant himself has brought before the court. (Id. at 864; see also Lantz v. Superior Court (1994) 28 Cal.App.4th 1839, 1853-1857 [court is required to balance patient's interest in her medical records against the stated need for the requested records].)

Here, just as in Hallendorf v. Superior Court (1978) 85 Cal.App.3d 553, the discovery sought by Defendants, which would require her physicians to disclose all treatment rendered to her over a period of 10 years without limitation to the medical conditions which she has put at issue, is "clearly overbroad." (Id. at 557.) As Plaintiff points out, the subpoenas are framed so broadly that they would encompass gynecological examinations or other medical diagnoses or treatments entirely unrelated to her claimed injuries. The subpoenas must be narrowed to seek only medical records and related documents pertaining to (i) Plaintiff's left shoulder and elbow, including any surgeries on or treatment of past dislocation of the shoulder, or (ii) Plaintiff's claimed mental and emotional injuries, both over the past ten years, a period which Plaintiff agrees is reasonable. If there is any further dispute regarding the discoverability of medical records called for by the subpoenas as narrowed, the parties may submit the records to the court for in camera review. (See, e.g., Kirchmeyer v. Phillips (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 1394, 1400 [trial court conducted in camera review of medical records and other related documents to determine whether patient's privacy interest in the documents outweighed the Medical Board's interest in their production].)

Defendant's refusal to narrow the scope of the subpoenas was not substantially justified in light of the long-standing authorities cited above. Accordingly, Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is granted in the amount of $1,000.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-13-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Michael Bracamontes, Esq. 415-835-6777 of Bracamontes & Vlasak for Plaintiff.
Christopher Whitman, Deputy Cty Attorney, 415-554-3800 of the Office of the City Attorney for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, SEAN NEARY'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the first cause of action for negligence in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Susan Bell is sustained WITH leave to amend but only as to a non-hiring, training and disciplining claim.

Ms. Bell alleges that the City is directly and vicariously liable for the negligent training, hiring, and disciplining of its officers. The City has no direct liability for negligent hiring and supervision. (de Villers v. County of San Diego (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 238, 252 ["We find no relevant case law approving a claim for direct liability based on a public entity's allegedly negligent hiring and supervision practices."]; Munoz v. City of Union City (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 1077, 1110-1113 [city had no direct liability in police shooting case based on negligence in the selection, training, retention, supervision, and discipline of police officers], disapproved on other grounds, Hayes v. County of San Diego (2013) 57 Cal.4th 622, 639 fn. 1.) A claim against the City for negligent hiring and supervision could be based on vicarious liability for the actions of its employees only where there is a special relationship such that liability should attach. "Absent such a special relationship, there can be no individual liability to third parties for negligent hiring, retention or supervision of a fellow employee, and hence no vicarious liability under section 815.2" (C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 877.)

Ms. Bell has not alleged a special relationship with the City, nor has she shown that there is a reasonable possibility that she can do so. Giraldo v. Department of Corrections & Rehabilitation (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 231 is inapposite. That case recognized a special relationship between a jailer and a prisoner giving rise to a duty of care to protect the prisoner from foreseeable harm inflicted by a third party, stemming from the vulnerability and dependence of a person in custody. Ms. Bell's situation does not present the same attributes of vulnerability and dependence. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/HEK)

Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to discovery responses from defendant is granted on the following conditions: (1) the Court will enter the parties' stipulated confidentiality order, which the parties shall submit at or prior to the hearing; (2) the City shall produce the body worn camera videos, without further redactions, and shall designate those portions of the videos that it contends are entitled to confidential treatment under applicable law; (3) the City shall provide a description of any complaints it received concerning police use of excessive force relating to the clearing operation on July 9, 2018, regardless of the extent of any injury allegedly suffered; (4) the City shall provide the number of complaints of use of excessive force, regardless of the extent of any injury allegedly suffered, made in the last 10 years against Officer Jensen, Officer Neary, and the "presently unidentified officer(s) [who] moved Plaintiff closer to" Officers Jensen and Neary, if and when identified; (5) The parties shall meet and confer regarding mutually acceptable language for the City's discovery verifications.

Order signed in open court.
